Opinion
Maria Corina MachadoVenezuelaNicolas MaduroMaduroLatin AmericaTrump
opinion

Let Venezuela be Free at Last | Opinion

Published

Jan 27, 2026 at 08:00 AM EST

updated

Jan 27, 2026 at 08:01 AM EST

By John Davenport

Professor of Philosophy, Fordham University

Newsweek is a Trust Project member

Among President Donald Trump’s many shocking actions in foreign affairs, his stance on Venezuela needs to get far more attention from the American public. That’s because, of all Trump’s crimes, his direct support for the tyranny in Venezuela is by far the worst.

I’m not referring to decisions by Trump and his Secretary of State Marco Rubio to remove de facto Venezuelan President Nicolás Maduro by force (Maduro lost both of the last two elections but remained in power through repression of his political opponents). There was plenty of justification for removing Maduro, although it should have been done by Latin American leaders—as I have argued previously.   

...

The giant problem is that Trump and Rubio have allied us with Delcy Rodríguez, the interim president of Venezuela who runs the same totally corrupt military-narco cabal that has disappeared and tortured thousands of innocent Venezuelans for over 15 years, subjecting many to sexual violence, totally destroying this once-rich nation’s economy and forcing almost 8 million Venezuelans to flee.

Rodríguez herself is suspected of drug trafficking and gold smuggling, along with money laundering, by the U.S. Drug Enforcement Administration (DEA). She has been integral to the mafia that has rigged Venezuela’s elections, terrorized its people and destroyed its democratic institutions. As the G.W. Bush Presidential Center noted, Rodríguez has cracked down even harder on independent journalists after her inauguration. Delcy’s brother Jorge Rodríguez also runs the sham parliament that Maduro put in power by force after the legitimately elected legislature rejected Maduro and proclaimed Juan Guaidó interim president in December 2018—in a plan approved by then-Secretary of State Mike Pompeo during Trump’s first term.

Rodríguez and her brother are the same totalitarian regime that Maduro headed—the tyranny which Trump’s own U.N. Ambassador Mike Waltz condemned for its crimes against humanity. Instead of ridding Venezuela of the vampires bleeding their nation dry, we are in bed with the same criminal network that is closely connected with Vladimir Putin, owes China $30 billion and is defended by special forces from the Cuban tyranny, 32 of whom were killed in the U.S. raid to seize Maduro.

And as long as Rodríguez’s paramilitary terror machine controls Venezuela, few Venezuelan refugees will return home; instead, more will leave. With no U.S. troops in Venezuela according to the administration, Rodríguez has a free hand.

So why is our government propping up Rodríguez, rather than turning over control of Venezuela to the true winners of Venezuela’s last election, namely democratic reformers Edmundo González, María Corina Machado and their party? Perhaps it is partly because Trump is so offended—as he has often said—that Machado received a Nobel Peace Prize that should have gone to him (despite his saber-rattling toward Greenland and his refusal to help Ukraine stop Vladimir Putin’s war of aggression).

In desperation to help her long-suffering people, Machado recently gave Trump her Nobel medal, before she was hurried out the back entrance.

But there are far darker reasons for Trump’s support of Rodríguez. For one, she organized a $500,000 donation to his 2025 inauguration event. And as an AP investigation revealed, she has worked for years to establish close allies within U.S. oil corporations.

In a lame attempt to justify this sickening spectacle, Trump has argued that putting in Machado would dispossess the Venezuelan military who hold their nation in thrall, and thereby repeat the mistake we made in Iraq 2003 by throwing all of Saddam Hussein’s forces out of a job. This is utter nonsense. Cleaning house in Venezuela will require purging the top officers, not everyone on the government payroll. “Stability” in Venezuela neither requires nor justifies a totalitarian regime ruling through black shirt thugs on the streets of Caracas.

G.W. Bush made several big mistakes in Iraq to be sure. But there’s one far worse mistake that Bush could never have dreamed of making—namely to put one of Saddam’s blood-drenched sons or henchmen in charge of Iraq while we harvest the oil. I’m sure Bush would have been impeached if he did that. Yet when Trump does the equivalent today, it does not faze today’s slavish Republican congressional leaders.

Imagine how it looks to everyone across Latin America for the United States to set up and support a new military dictator as our puppet—the same crime that made our government infamous throughout the region for much of the 20th century. This is the best way conceivable to force democratic nations who should be our allies into the death grip of Russia and China. Americans thought we had rejected this imperialist nightmare over half a century ago.

Such an outrage really does call for the strongest protests and nonviolent civil disobedience possible. South Americans would be justified in imposing a total trade boycott on the United States until it is rectified.

This year, Americans will celebrate the 250th anniversary of the Declaration of Independence. If Trump and Rubio really care about the Declaration’s principles, then they must allow Machado and her unity party to take control in Venezuela. She is her people’s Thomas Jefferson. The United States can keep Venezuelan forces opposed to Machado in line while she reforms the military, rebuilds the infrastructure and restores the integrity of her nation’s public institutions.

And all the oil in the world is not worth one more day of military dictatorship in Venezuela.

John Davenport is professor of philosophy and director of peace and justice studies at Fordham University.

The views expressed in this article are the writer's own.
